elegant: Where does the human soul come from? Four inquiries into the origin of that which makes us more than dust: whether the soul is a fragment of God or something made, whether it was created from nothing, whether angels had a hand in fashioning it, and whether it existed before the body it animates.

The soul is not a piece of God. Flattering as the idea sounds, it collapses on contact with experience. We hesitate, we learn slowly, we possess faculties that war with one another. None of this is compatible with the divine nature, which is pure act, receives nothing from outside, and admits no internal division.

The error has ancient roots. Those who could conceive of nothing beyond the physical made God a body and the soul a fragment of it. The Manichaeans called God light and the soul a shard of that light trapped in flesh. Later thinkers recognized incorporeal reality but still could not distinguish levels of spiritual existence, so they made each soul a drop in one cosmic mind.

All these mistakes share a single flaw: they cannot account for what the soul actually does. A being that sometimes fails to understand, that acquires knowledge piece by piece, that contains competing powers, is not divine. It is something made.

The rational soul can come into existence only through creation from nothing. The logic is clean: how a thing is made must match how it exists. Only what subsists in its own right is truly "made." A quality like whiteness does not come into being independently; it arrives only when the white thing is produced. But the rational soul subsists. It is not merely a property of the body the way heat is a quality of fire.

Because it subsists, it can properly be said to exist and therefore to be made. And because it cannot be fashioned from any pre-existing matter, whether physical (which would make it a body) or spiritual (which would mean one spirit morphing into another), it can only come from the one source that needs no raw material at all. The soul is created, not generated. It enters existence the way only God can bring things into existence: from nothing.

Only God creates souls. The reasoning is as spare as the conclusion is sweeping. Creation means producing something from nothing. Every secondary cause works by transforming what already exists. The rational soul cannot be produced by transforming pre-existing matter. Therefore no created power, however exalted, can produce it. Angels can rearrange the furniture of the world, but they cannot add to it. Every rational soul is the direct and immediate work of God.

Was the soul created before the body it inhabits? Origen thought all souls were made with the angels, hovering in spiritual existence before descending into flesh. Augustine entertained the possibility without committing to it.

But the answer turns on what the soul is. If it were a complete being that merely administers the body from the outside, pre-existence would be conceivable. It is not. The soul is the body's form, naturally a part of human nature. A soul without a body is an incomplete human being. God made the first things in their natural completeness. The soul was not created before the body. It was created with it, as its natural fulfillment.

That the soul outlives the body proves nothing about pre-existence. Death is a defect that came later, not a feature of the original design. The soul survives the body because it is indestructible; it was created with the body because it is incomplete without one.
Four inquiries into the first human body: the matter from which it was formed, the hand that formed it, the fitness of its design, and the meaning of Scripture's account of its making.

Man is called a "little world" because every level of creation meets in him. His rational soul belongs to the order of spiritual substances. His balanced temperament echoes the stability of celestial bodies. And all four elements compose his flesh. The higher elements, fire and air, dominate in power, for life requires heat and moisture. The lower, earth and water, dominate in quantity, because weaker forces must be present in greater proportion to maintain equilibrium. Earth and water mixed are called slime. From slime, God fashioned the body that would house the universe in miniature.

Why earth and not something nobler? Because God's power was already displayed in creating matter from nothing. The body was made of all four elements so that man would share something with every stratum of physical creation. Celestial matter cannot form sense organs. If fire and air dominated in quantity as well as power, they would overwhelm the mixture and destroy the equilibrium that touch, the most fundamental sense, requires. The body is not made of the noblest substance but of the most fitting one. Design follows purpose, not prestige.

The first human body was made directly by God. No created power could have produced it. Forms in matter arise only when one composite generates another of the same kind, and no prior human body existed. Angels can rearrange existing material but cannot introduce new forms from nothing. God alone, being absolutely immaterial, can produce form in matter without precedent. The first human body had no template, no ancestor, no natural precursor. It required the one cause that needs none.

The claim that celestial bodies could have produced man overreaches. Even Aristotle's dictum that "man and the sun beget man" only means celestial power cooperates with natural generation, not that it replaces it. Forming a human body from earth is not a natural process; it is a supernatural act, like raising the dead. Nature assisted nothing here. The first body was entirely God's work.

The human body is not the most powerful or the most armored, but it is the best designed for what it must do: serve a rational soul. Every artist builds for the intended function. A saw is made of iron, not glass, though glass is more beautiful, because beauty would defeat the purpose. God fashioned the human body as the finest possible instrument of thought. Its apparent deficiencies are not flaws but trade-offs, the necessary costs of optimizing for intelligence.

Why do dogs smell better and hawks see farther? Because the human body is optimized for something else entirely: the most balanced temperament of any animal, which the sense of touch requires, and the largest brain relative to body size, which intellectual life demands. That large, moist brain dulls smell. That balanced temperament costs speed. Every gain in one direction is a calculated concession in another.

Why no claws, no armor, no thick hide? Because man has reason and hands, which Aristotle called "the instrument of instruments." A fixed biological weapon serves one purpose. Hands guided by intelligence serve infinite purposes.

Why upright posture? Because human senses exist for knowledge, not just survival. The upright face surveys heaven and earth, gathering raw material for understanding. The elevated brain houses the interior powers of thought. Free hands, unburdened from locomotion, become universal tools. And the mouth, liberated from the need to grasp food from the ground, becomes the organ of speech, reason's proper instrument.

Man's orientation is itself significant. Head toward heaven, feet toward earth. Plants are inverted. Animals are horizontal. Only man is aligned between the higher and lower worlds.

Scripture's special language for the creation of man is deliberate. "Let us make" signals not divine need but divine deliberation, reflecting the plurality of Persons whose image is most fully expressed in the human creature. "He breathed into his face the breath of life" is not a separate event from forming the body; it is the explanation of what makes the body alive. The soul is the form of the body. The face is named because that is where life's operations are most visible.

Body and soul were not produced in sequence. Each is only a part of human nature; neither is complete alone, and the body depends on the soul rather than the reverse. To make one without the other would have been unfitting for the Author of complete natures.
Four questions attend the creation of woman: whether she should have been made at all, whether from man, whether from his rib specifically, and whether directly by God.

Woman was necessary, but as a partner in generation, not as a general assistant. For any other kind of work, another man would have been more efficient. The distinction between male and female follows the logic of living things. In the simplest organisms, reproduction is built into every individual. In plants, both functions are permanently united. In perfect animals, active and passive generative powers separate into distinct sexes because animal life has purposes beyond reproduction. In humans, the separation is most pronounced because human life is ordered to the highest purpose of all: thought. The female was produced separately from the male so that each could devote the fullness of their nature to intellectual life, united in flesh only for the work of generation.

Aristotle's claim that woman is a "defective male" applies only at the level of individual generation, where the male seed aims to produce its own likeness and sometimes does not. At the level of nature's universal intention, woman is not defective but deliberately included. God intends both sexes.

Subjection has two forms. Servile subjection, where one exploits another for personal advantage, began only after sin. Ordered governance, where the more capable direct others for the common good, would have existed even in paradise. Any community requires leadership. Inequality is not inherently punishment.

The objection that God should not have made woman because she would occasion sin proves far too much. Remove everything that becomes an occasion of sin, and the universe would be imperfect. The common good is not sacrificed to prevent individual evil.

It was fitting that woman be made from man. Four reasons converge. First, it gave Adam a dignity analogous to God's: as God is the principle of the universe, man was made the principle of the entire human race. Second, it deepened the bond between them. Knowing she came from him, he would cleave to her more closely. Third, human marriage is not merely reproductive but domestic, a lifelong partnership of complementary roles. Her origin from man reflects this ordered union. Fourth, there is a sacramental dimension: the Church springs from Christ as woman sprang from man.

Divine power is not bound by the determinism of natural generation. God can produce the same species from any matter.

From the rib, not the head, not the feet. Not from the head, lest she dominate. Not from the feet, lest she be despised. From the side, signifying partnership. And from the side of Christ on the cross flowed blood and water, the sacraments on which the Church was built.

The rib was not stretched to become a woman. Divine power supplied additional matter, as Christ multiplied loaves not by stretching bread but by creating substance. The rib belonged to Adam's perfection not as an individual but as the principle of the race, the way seed belongs to the one who generates. Its removal caused no pain, for pain belongs to the fallen state.

Woman was formed directly by God. Natural generation produces individuals from species-specific matter through the power already present in that species. But producing a human body from a rib exceeds every created power, just as producing one from earth does. Only the Author of nature can work outside nature's ordinary course. Angels may have assisted in minor ways. The actual formation was God's alone.

The purpose of human creation is that we are made to the image and likeness of God. Nine questions probe what this means: whether the image truly exists in us, whether it extends to animals, whether angels bear it more perfectly, whether every person carries it, whether it reflects the Trinity or only the divine essence, whether it resides in the mind alone, whether it lives in acts or powers, whether it requires God as its object, and how "image" and "likeness" differ.

An image requires two things: likeness and derivation. One egg resembles another but is not its image, because it was not copied from it. A reflection is an image because it is derived from the original. Equality is not required, though a perfect image would lack nothing found in what it represents.

Man bears a genuine likeness to God, derived from God as exemplar. But the copy infinitely falls short of the original. This is why Scripture says man was made "to" God's image, with the preposition signaling approach from a distance, not arrival. Christ is the Image of the Father, being of identical nature. Man is "to the image," an analogy sketched in a lesser substance, the way a king's likeness appears on a silver coin.

The image of God is found only in creatures that think. A generic resemblance or a shared accidental feature is not enough. Things resemble God in three ascending degrees: by existing, by living, and by understanding. Only the last approaches close enough to qualify as an image.

A worm may descend from a man but is not his image, because the likeness is too remote. The whole universe surpasses any individual creature in collective goodness, but the intellectual creature surpasses everything else in the intensity and specificity of its likeness to God. The image is found where God's defining characteristic, intellect, is specifically mirrored.

Angels bear God's image more perfectly than we do, because the image consists primarily in intellectual nature, and angelic intellect surpasses human intellect. There are secondary respects in which man resembles God more closely: man proceeds from man as God proceeds from God; the soul pervades the body as God pervades the world. But these secondary likenesses only count as aspects of the divine image if the primary one, intellect, is already present. Otherwise even animals would qualify. Since the defining criterion is intellectual nature, and angels excel in it, they are more fully God's image. Man surpasses them only in the margins.

Every human being bears the image of God, because every human being has an intellectual nature. But the image operates at three depths. First, the image of creation: every person has the natural capacity to understand and love God. This belongs to all. Second, the image of re-creation: those who actually know and love God through grace. This belongs to the just. Third, the image of glory: those who know and love God perfectly. This belongs to the blessed.

Both men and women bear the image equally. It resides in the mind, where there is no sexual distinction.

Does the image of God in us reflect the Trinity of Persons, or only the single divine essence? Some argue it can only reflect what is common to all three Persons, since the attributes we imitate, intelligence, freedom, goodness, belong to the one nature. Others worry that if the image reflected the Trinity, we could know the Trinity by natural reason alone, which we cannot.

The image reflects both the divine nature and the Trinity, because the two are inseparable. The distinction of Persons arises from the divine nature through relations of origin. To imitate the nature is already, implicitly, to represent the Persons.

The objection that this would make the Trinity knowable by reason alone fails because the image in us is deeply imperfect. As Augustine observed, we see the trinity within ourselves; we only believe the Trinity in God. The gap between our interior life of thought and love and the divine processions is vast enough that the image delivers suggestion, not demonstration.

Is the image of God spread across the whole person, body and soul alike? Paul calls "the man" God's image. Genesis ties the image to the creation of male and female. Shape seems essential to what an image is. Yet Paul also says renewal "according to the image" consists in putting on the new man in the spirit of the mind. The image belongs to the mind specifically.

In other creatures, and in the non-rational parts of the human being, we find only a trace of God, not an image. The distinction is precise. An image represents by likeness in species. A trace represents the way an effect points to a cause without reaching specific likeness: footprints trace an animal, ashes trace a fire, desolation traces an army.

Rational creatures achieve a representation of species because they imitate God not only in being and living but in understanding. In the mind's procession of word from knowledge and love from both, we find a distant but real mirror of the Word proceeding from the Father and Love from both. In everything below the mind, even in the human body's upright posture, the likeness to God is by trace only. The image lives in the mind alone.

The image is not distributed across every part of man but is impressed on his mind, as a coin bears the king's likeness without every particle of metal being the portrait. The distinction of male and female does not constitute the image; Scripture mentions both sexes immediately after mentioning the image precisely to show it belongs to both, since it resides where there is no sexual distinction.

The trinities found in sense perception and imagination fall short of the divine image because their components are external or borrowed. Only the mind's own acts of understanding and love, arising from within its own nature, approach the internal processions of the divine Persons.

The image of the Trinity lives most fully in the soul's acts, not merely in its powers or habits. The divine Persons are distinguished by the procession of Word from Speaker and Love from both. In us, a word cannot exist without actual thought. Therefore the image is most present when we think, produce an interior word from that thinking, and break forth from both into love.

Powers and habits are the springs from which acts flow. The image exists in them secondarily, as a fire exists virtually in the fuel. But the primary locus is the act itself: the living moment of knowledge and love.

Augustine's trinities of mind-knowledge-love and memory-understanding-will both point toward the image, with the latter being more precise. The key insight: the image resides most fully in actual thought and actual love, not in the habitual retention of knowledge. Even when the mind is not actively thinking, it always remembers, understands, and loves itself habitually. The image exists permanently in the habits as latent fire, and fully when those habits kindle into act.

The image is most truly present when the mind turns toward God. Different objects produce specifically different words and loves. The Word of God is born of divine self-knowledge; divine Love proceeds from divine self-love. The truest image in us is the word born of knowing God and the love that flows from that knowledge.

When the mind knows itself, it indirectly mirrors God, since self-knowledge is a stepping stone to knowing the one who made it. But knowledge of temporal things, however trinitarian in structure, does not constitute the image. The image requires the mind to engage with what is above it, not below.

For the image to hold, what proceeds in us must be a word of God proceeding from knowledge of God. A mere trinity of cognitive elements about any object is not sufficient.

Even so, the image never fully vanishes. The natural capacity to know God always remains, whether "obsolete" in those without reason's use, "obscured" in sinners, or "clear and beautiful" in the just. In the beatific vision, even temporal things will be seen in God and through God's unchangeable truth. At that point, the contemplation of all things will belong to the image, because everything will be known through the divine Word.

"Image" and "likeness" are not redundant. Likeness is the genus; image is the species. Wherever there is an image there is likeness, but not the reverse.

The two can be distinguished in two ways. First, likeness as preamble to image: likeness covers the more general features (the soul's incorruptibility, its simplicity) while the image proper is found specifically in intellectual nature. In this sense, the mind bears God's image while the body and lower faculties bear only God's likeness.

Second, likeness as the perfection of image: an image is "like" its original when the representation succeeds, "unlike" when it falls short. In this sense, likeness of power and virtue tells you how well the image is being used. The image gives you the structure. The likeness tells you whether the structure is fulfilled.

Likeness is not distinct from image in the general sense, since every image involves likeness. It is distinct in the specific sense: either as falling short of the image (covering features too broad to constitute it) or as perfecting it (measuring how well the image is realized). The soul's essence belongs to the image insofar as it represents the divine essence in intellectual nature. Love of the word, knowledge beloved, belongs to image. Love of virtue belongs to likeness. The two terms map the same reality from different angles.
The first man's mind: four questions. Did Adam see God as God sees Himself? Could he perceive angels directly? Did he possess all knowledge? And was he capable of error?

Adam did not see God in His essence. The proof is devastatingly simple: anyone who sees God's essence possesses beatitude, and no one possessing beatitude can willingly turn from God. Adam sinned. Therefore he did not see God's essence.

But he knew God far more clearly than we do, in a way midway between our clouded present knowledge and the unveiled vision of heaven. We know God through sensible things, and our engagement with the sensible world constantly distracts us from higher contemplation. In Adam, the lower powers were perfectly subject to reason. Nothing in his nature dragged him downward. He could contemplate God's intelligible effects with a clarity and steadiness we cannot achieve, receiving illumination from the First Truth as directly as a mind still bound to images can receive it.

Adam was happy in paradise, but not with the final happiness of seeing God face to face. He possessed a genuine, if incomplete, blessedness: the natural integrity and perfection of an unfallen creature. His will was well-ordered precisely because it did not grasp at the final reward before the time of merit was completed. He knew God without the obscurity that sin imposes, but not without the inherent limitation of knowing God through creatures rather than directly.

Adam could not see angels in their essence. The human soul, even unfallen, is adapted to govern the body and therefore naturally understands by turning to images drawn from sense experience. This mode of knowing proceeds in three movements: inward from external things to self-knowledge, upward toward association with higher intelligences, and higher still toward God.

The first movement yields genuine self-knowledge. The second falls short of knowing angels directly, because angelic thought operates on entirely different principles. Knowing how we ourselves think tells us nothing about what it means to think as an angel does. The third movement falls even shorter of knowing God.

Adam knew angels better than we do, because his intellectual life was clearer and less obstructed. But "better" is not "directly." The gap between human and angelic cognition remained.

Innocence did not change the soul's nature. It removed corruption while preserving natural integrity. The soul still understood by turning to sense-derived images. Adam's inability to know angels directly was not because his body burdened his soul (as ours does) but because the soul's natural cognitive object simply falls short of angelic reality. We suffer both limitations. Adam suffered only the second.

Adam possessed knowledge of everything humans can naturally know, plus the supernatural truths necessary for directing human life. The reasoning: perfection precedes imperfection, act precedes potentiality. God created things not only for their own existence but as principles of other things. The first man was the father and teacher of the whole human race. He needed comprehensive knowledge from the start.

What he did not know: other people's private thoughts, future contingent events, trivial particulars like the number of pebbles in a stream. What he would have gained over time: not new truths, but experiential confirmation of what he already knew theoretically. His knowledge came through divinely infused species, but it was not different in kind from natural knowledge, as miraculously given eyes are no different from natural ones.

In the state of innocence, the intellect could not assent to falsehood. Truth is the good of the intellect; falsehood is its evil. In a state where no evil existed, no false judgment could exist either.

The deeper reason is structural. The rectitude of the original state meant the soul was subject to God and the lower faculties subject to reason. Deception never comes from the intellect itself, which regarding its proper object is always true. It comes from interference by imagination, sensation, or desire. When reason's natural judgment operates freely and undistorted, we are not deceived by phantasms. A waking person is not fooled by dream-images. In the state of innocence, reason was always fully awake.

Eve was deceived, but not before she sinned. She could not have believed the serpent unless she had already given way to pride in her own heart. The external deception followed the internal fall, not the reverse.

If something false had been presented to Adam's senses, his reason would have judged correctly. During sleep, when reason is suspended, he was not accountable. If someone had told him something false about future events, he would not have believed it was so but might have considered it possible, which is not a false opinion. In every case, either reason would have corrected the error or divine guidance would have prevented it. After he sinned in his heart, that guidance was forfeit.

The first man's will, examined in two dimensions: his grace and righteousness, and the dominion that flowed from them. Four questions open the first: Was Adam created in grace? Did he have passions? Did he possess all virtues? Were his actions less meritorious than ours?

Adam was created in grace, not in bare nature. The argument is structural and irreversible. The rectitude of the original state consisted in reason being subject to God, the lower powers subject to reason, and the body subject to the soul. The first subjection caused the other two. When reason's submission to God was broken by sin, the lower powers immediately rebelled.

Now the subjection of body to soul and passion to reason was clearly not natural, because natural gifts survive sin, as they did even in the fallen angels. Therefore the primary subjection that caused these others was also not natural but supernatural, a gift of grace. An effect cannot exceed its cause. If the loss of grace dissolved the body's obedience, grace must have been what sustained it. Adam was created with grace from the first moment of his existence.

Paul's contrast between Adam as "living soul" and Christ as "life-giving spirit" refers to the body's mode of existence, not the soul's spiritual condition. Adam had spiritual life in his soul through grace; what he lacked was the glorified, spiritual body that Christ inaugurates. He possessed the Holy Spirit, though not in the way the blessed do who enter eternal happiness immediately after death.

Grace was given not to nature as such but to the person within nature. Man was created in grace, but it was by virtue of that grace, not the nature in which he was created, that he could advance by merit.

Adam had passions, but only certain kinds and in a distinctive mode. He had no fear, because no evil threatened. No sorrow, because no evil was present. No burning concupiscence, because no good was denied. But he had joy, love, measured desire, and hope that does not disappoint.

The crucial difference between then and now: in our present state, passions often run ahead of reason or resist it. In Adam, the sensory appetite was entirely governed by reason. Passions arose only as consequences of rational judgment, never as disruptions of it. This was not the absence of feeling but its perfect ordering. As Aristotle noted, the temperate person desires what is appropriate, in the appropriate way, at the appropriate time.

Adam possessed all the virtues, but in a carefully calibrated way. The rectitude of the original state required that reason be subject to God and the lower powers subject to reason. Virtues are precisely the perfections that accomplish this. So Adam had every one.

Virtues that involve no inherent imperfection, like charity and justice, existed fully in both habit and act. Virtues whose imperfection is compatible with innocence, like faith and hope, also existed in habit and act, since Adam had not yet seen God face to face. But virtues whose imperfection is incompatible with innocence, like penance and mercy, existed only as dispositions, never activated. He was so disposed that he would have repented if there had been cause, and would have helped anyone in need if need had existed. The disposition was real; only the occasion was absent.

Temperance and fortitude do not essentially require excess to subdue. They moderate passions of any intensity. In innocence, they operated on well-ordered passions rather than disordered ones. Passions regarding evil in another, like hating the demons' malice, were compatible with innocence. Passions regarding evil in oneself, like sorrow over personal suffering, were not.

Adam had perseverance as a virtue, the habitual choice to continue in good, but not perseverance as an accomplished fact, since he eventually fell. The virtuous person may possess the disposition to remain standing without in fact remaining standing forever.

Were Adam's actions less meritorious than ours, since he faced no struggle? Merit can be measured two ways. By its root in grace and charity, Adam's works were more meritorious: his grace met no obstacle and was more copious. Absolutely, his works were greater because his virtue was greater.

But proportionally, our works may merit more: a small deed done with great difficulty exceeds our capacity more dramatically than a great deed done effortlessly exceeds the capacity of someone strong. The widow's mite outweighs the rich man's gold in proportion to what each possesses.

Adam's advantage was absolute. Ours is relative. We need grace for more things, but we do not need it more. Difficulty increases proportional merit, but it does not make our actions simply greater than those of an unfallen will operating at full strength.
Human dominion in the state of innocence: four questions. Was man master of the animals? Of all creatures? Were all people equal? Would some have governed others?

Before sin, all animals were subject to man. Three arguments converge. First, the order of nature: the imperfect exists for the use of the perfect. Plants use earth, animals use plants, man uses all. Second, divine providence: God governs the lower through the higher. Man, made in God's image, is the natural governor of what lacks that image. Third, the gap between universal and particular reason: animals have instinctive prudence for specific situations; man has universal practical reason for all situations. What is partial is naturally subject to what is comprehensive.

Before sin, man had not forfeited this authority through disobedience. The rebellion of creatures against human rule is the mirror of human rebellion against God. The disorder we observe now is consequence, not original condition.

Predatory animals were always predatory. Sin did not change animal nature. Lions did not eat grass before the Fall. But natural antipathy between species did not exempt them from human governance, just as it does not exempt them from divine providence now. In innocence, man had no bodily need of animals, neither for clothing (they were naked without shame) nor for food (they ate from the trees) nor for transport (the body was strong enough). He needed them for one thing: knowledge. Animals would have obeyed voluntarily through their natural participation in instinctive prudence, as domestic animals obey now.

Man's dominion over creation mirrored his dominion over himself. He contains all levels of reality: reason, sensation, vegetative life, and material body. Over angels, he had no authority; reason is not their master. Over animals, he ruled by command, since the sensitive powers obey reason. Over plants and inanimate things, he ruled not by commanding but by using, since vegetative processes and physical matter do not respond to commands but can be employed without hindrance. The course of the heavenly bodies he could not alter. That belongs to God alone.

Even in paradise, perfect equality would not have existed. At minimum, diversity of sex and age would have obtained. Beyond these, free will would have produced differences in virtue and knowledge, since people would have applied themselves in varying degrees. Bodies would have differed in robustness, beauty, and size, shaped by climate, nutrition, and other natural factors.

But none of these inequalities would have involved defect or fault. The differences would have been in degree of excellence, not between excellence and deficiency. Order requires inequality. As Augustine says, order arranges equal and unequal things in their proper place. The beauty of ordered diversity was part of the original design, not a punishment for its failure.

There are two kinds of authority. Servile authority, where one person uses another for his own advantage, is inherently grievous and began only after sin. No one willingly yields what should be his own.

But governance for the common good, directing free people toward their own welfare and the welfare of all, would have existed even in innocence. Two reasons compel this. First, man is naturally social, and any society requires someone to attend to the common interest. Left to themselves, many individuals pursue many different ends; one person looking after the whole is essential. Second, natural differences in knowledge and virtue would have been wasted if they did not benefit others.

Just men command not through love of domination but through the service of counsel. In paradise, leadership would have been the purest form of service.
The first man's body, examined in four aspects: Was he immortal? Was he immune to suffering? Did he need food? And what role did the tree of life play in preserving him?

Man was immortal in the state of innocence, but not by nature. His body was not intrinsically incorruptible like an angel, which has no matter, or a celestial body, whose matter admits only one form. God gave the soul a supernatural power to preserve the body from corruption, effective only so long as the soul remained subject to God.

This makes perfect sense. The rational soul surpasses the capacity of corporeal matter; it was fitting that it received a power surpassing what matter could naturally sustain. When that supernatural gift was forfeited through sin, the body's natural corruptibility reasserted itself. Death entered through disobedience, not through nature.

This immortality differed from the immortality of glory promised as a reward. The first was conditional, maintained by grace as long as obedience continued. The second will be permanent and absolute.

"Passion" in the strict sense means being altered away from your natural disposition. Adam could sense, sleep, and undergo natural processes, but nothing contrary to his natural well-being could touch him. He could control passion as he could avoid death, so long as he refrained from sin.

His body was protected from external harm partly by his own reason and partly by divine providence, ensuring nothing dangerous came upon him unawares. The removal of the rib was not a natural deterioration but was analogous to the provision of seed: material given for the generation of the species, not a loss to the individual.

Adam needed food. His immortality was not intrinsic but sustained by a supernatural power in the soul. Natural heat still consumed the body's moisture, and food was required to replace what was lost. In the primitive state, man had an animal life requiring nourishment; after the resurrection, he will have a spiritual life needing none.

The soul has two identities. As soul, what it shares with other living things, it gives animal life requiring the vegetative functions of nutrition, growth, and generation. As spirit, what is unique to it, it will eventually communicate immortality, glory, and power to the body. In paradise, the first mode governed. After resurrection, the second will.

The tree of life was genuinely medicinal but not a source of absolute immortality. The body faced two threats: the ongoing loss of moisture to natural heat (remedied by ordinary food from the other trees), and the gradual weakening of the body's assimilative power over time, the process we call aging, where added nutrients progressively dilute the original vital force like water added to wine.

The tree of life addressed the second threat, strengthening the body's vital force against progressive decay. But its power was finite. It could preserve the body for a definite time, after which man would either eat from it again or be transferred to the spiritual life. The soul's supernatural power of preservation remained the primary cause; the tree was a bodily supplement against one specific mode of decline.
Reproduction in the state of innocence: two questions. Would generation have occurred at all? And would it have been through sexual union?

Generation in innocence would have been through sexual union. Some early thinkers, troubled by the nature of concupiscence, imagined the human race would have multiplied by divine power alone, as angels do. But what is natural to man by virtue of his animal life was neither acquired nor lost through sin. The body's reproductive design is evident. These organs would not have been purposeless before the Fall.

Two elements must be distinguished. First, the natural union of male and female with their complementary generative powers, which belongs to the order of nature and would have existed in innocence. Second, the disordered excess of concupiscence that currently accompanies the act, which is a consequence of the lower powers' rebellion against reason and would not have existed.

As Augustine says: the reproductive organs would have been moved by the will like any other limb, without ardent or disordered incentive, with calmness of soul and body.

The comparison to angels fails. In paradise, man was angel-like in mind but still animal in body. Only after the resurrection will both be spiritualized.

What makes sexual behavior bestial is not the act but the absence of rational control. In innocence, reason would not have diminished pleasure. Augustine suggests it would have been greater, with purer nature and keener sensitivity. But concupiscence would not have dominated. A sober person enjoys food no less than a glutton. The difference is that the glutton's appetite controls him.

Continence would not have been praiseworthy in that state, because there would have been nothing disordered to restrain. Virginal integrity would not have been compromised by the act. Conception and birth would have been free of the distortions that accompany them now. The union would have been one of deliberate action, not lustful urgency.
Two questions about the bodies of children born in innocence: Would they have had full physical strength from birth? And would all children have been male?

Children would not have possessed adult strength at birth, but neither would they have suffered the helplessness we observe in infants now. It is natural for human newborns to lack strength because the brain is proportionally large and its high moisture content impedes the nerves that control movement.

The original righteousness meant the body could do nothing contrary to the well-ordered will. But a well-ordered will desires only what is fitting for its stage of life. An infant's will would not reach for adult actions. Children would have had strength enough for the activities of infancy but not for everything an adult can do.

Infancy's weakness is natural and follows from birth. Old age's weakness leads to corruption. Man in innocence was born but not subject to corruption. The limitations of beginning would have existed; the decline of ending would not.

Both sexes would have been born. Diversity of sex belongs to the perfection of human nature, just as different levels of being belong to the perfection of the universe. Aristotle's claim that woman is a "misbegotten male" refers only to the individual case. At the level of universal nature, the generation of females is fully intended.

In innocence, where the body was more subject to the soul, parents might have been able to determine the sex of their children by will alone. Since all children would have needed to reproduce, both sexes in roughly equal numbers would have been necessary.
The moral condition of children in innocence: two questions. Would they have been born righteous? And would they have been born confirmed in righteousness, unable to sin?

Children would have been born in a state of original righteousness, including sanctifying grace. The argument turns on what original righteousness is: not a personal trait of Adam's but an endowment of the entire human nature, a gift pertaining to the species. This is clear from the fact that its opposite, original sin, is transmitted as a condition of nature. If the corruption passes through nature, the righteousness would have as well.

The grace would not have been transmitted biologically. God would have conferred it directly on each soul at the moment of its creation, just as the rational soul itself is not generated by the parents but infused by God when the body is ready. Nature provides the body. God provides the soul and its grace.

Children would not have been born confirmed in righteousness. Confirmation comes only through the beatific vision: once a rational creature sees God as He is, it cannot turn away, because God is the essence of goodness and nothing is desired except under the aspect of good. But Adam had not yet attained the beatific vision, and had he done so, his animal life, and with it the capacity for generation, would have ceased.

So no children could have been born to parents already confirmed. Children, like their parents, could have sinned by their own free will. Their preservation from sin would have depended on divine providence and their own choices, not on an irrevocable state. The angel analogy does not apply: angelic free will becomes fixed after its first choice, while human free will remains changeable throughout mortal life.
Two questions about the knowledge of children born in innocence: Would they have entered the world with perfect knowledge? And would they have had full use of reason from the moment of birth?

Children would not have been born knowing everything. The soul naturally acquires knowledge through the senses; that is why it is united to a body in the first place. If the soul arrived fully equipped with knowledge, the body would serve no cognitive purpose, which contradicts the very reason for the union.

Adam's comprehensive knowledge was a special endowment for his unique role as father and teacher of humanity, not a feature of human nature as such. His children would have inherited the species' natural and gracious gifts but not his individual privileges. They would have learned over time, easily and without the obstacles we face.

This would not have been ignorance. Ignorance is the absence of knowledge that should be present. Children would have possessed whatever knowledge was appropriate to their stage. Even the holy angels have nescience of things not yet revealed to them.

Children would not have had full use of reason at birth. The use of reason depends on the sensitive powers, which depend on bodily organs, and in newborns the brain's high moisture content impedes neural function. This is a natural condition, not a punishment.

Nature proceeds from imperfect to perfect. Even in innocence, human development would have followed this arc. Some animals walk at birth, but this reflects simpler neural demands, not greater perfection. The most complex organisms need the most time to develop. Children in innocence would have had more use of reason than infants do now, but still only what their stage of growth allowed.
Paradise: four questions. Was it a physical place? Was it suited to human habitation? Why was man placed there? And was man created there, or brought there afterward?

Paradise was a real, physical place. Scripture narrates events there as history: the planting of trees, the formation of Eve, the rivers that flowed through it. Historical narrative demands a real location. The strongest view, which Augustine endorsed, holds that paradise was both corporeal and spiritual: a genuine garden in the east, situated in the most excellent part of the earth, carrying also a spiritual significance that does not cancel its physical reality.

The tree of life was a real tree with genuine life-preserving power. The tree of knowledge was equally real, named not for a magical property but for what would result from eating it: the experiential knowledge of the difference between obedience and rebellion.

The claim that paradise reached the moon should not be taken literally. It may mean its atmosphere had the constant, temperate quality we associate with celestial bodies. The rivers may have flowed underground before emerging at their known sources, a common phenomenon. The reason no explorer has found it is that impassable barriers, mountains, seas, or extreme climate, cut it off from the habitable world. Inaccessibility does not disprove existence.

Paradise was perfectly fitted to man's original state. The human body faced two kinds of corruption: internal aging and external environmental damage. Paradise addressed both. Its temperate, pure atmosphere shielded against external assault. Its trees, including the tree of life, nourished against internal decay.

Why not the empyrean heaven, where angels dwell? Because it suits angelic nature, which is purely spiritual and governs all corporeal creation from above, and the state of beatitude, which is maximally stable. Man's nature is not purely spiritual, and his original state was not yet beatitude. Paradise suited man as he was: embodied, immortal by grace, and on the way toward beatitude rather than already possessing it.

The empyrean heaven was man's destination, not his starting point. Paradise was adapted to a composite being of soul and body, providing what the body needed precisely because the soul's preserving power worked through bodily conditions.

No irrational animal naturally inhabited paradise, though God brought animals there for Adam to study and the serpent entered through diabolical agency. Paradise did not become useless after man's expulsion. It stands as testimony to divine generosity and a measure of what was lost.

Man was placed in paradise "to dress it and keep it." Read one way: God worked in man and kept him, sanctifying and preserving him, since without God's ongoing work the soul falls into darkness as air grows dark when light withdraws. Read another way: man tended the garden, but this labor would have been pleasant, not toilsome, a form of delighted knowledge rather than grudging work. And "keeping" paradise meant keeping it for himself by not sinning, not guarding it against trespassers.

Paradise was for man. Man was not for paradise.

Man was not created in paradise but was made outside it and placed there by God. The arrangement carries a theological truth: the blessed condition was God's gift, not something inherent in human nature. By making man elsewhere and transferring him, God made clear that the incorruptibility of the original state was supernatural, not natural.

Angels were created in their proper abode because the empyrean suits their nature directly. For man, paradise suited his supernatural endowment, not his bare nature. Woman was made in paradise not for her own dignity but because her material, Adam's rib, was already there. Children would have been born there because their parents already were. The place followed the gift, not the nature.
